headline: Adam Smith’s ‘Wealth of Nations’ Helped Create the World’s Wealthiest Nation
summary: “Every man, as long as he does not violate the laws of justice, is left perfectly free to pursue his own interest his own way, and to bring both his industry and his capital into competition with those of any other man, or order of men,” Adam Smith wrote in , published March 9, 1776.  Thomas Jefferson didn’t get a chance to read Wealth of Nations before starting on the Declaration of Independence two months later.  Jefferson’s assault was political, arguing that a free people had the right to reject a tyrannical king.

content.title: Adam Smith’s ‘Wealth of Nations’ Helped Create the World’s Wealthiest Nation
content.summary: “Every man, as long as he does not violate the laws of justice, is left perfectly free to pursue his own interest his own way, and to bring both his industry and his capital into competition with those of any other man, or order of men,” Adam Smith wrote in , published March 9, 1776.  Thomas Jefferson didn’t get a chance to read Wealth of Nations before starting on the Declaration of Independence two months later.  Jefferson’s assault was political, arguing that a free people had the right to reject a tyrannical king.

headline: Intel results to test if AI-fueled rally has room to run
summary: Intel's results on Thursday will show whether the American chip icon has the numbers to back a Wall Street rally that has ‌sent its shares nearly three times higher this year, as its turnaround ‌push wins over investors and the AI buildout powers demand.  Investors are also eager for details on potential new customers for its contract manufacturing business, which has shown growing traction with deals including a ‌Tesla tie-up.  • Among the biggest ⁠questions for Intel is a potential deal to manufacture processors for Apple that U.S. President Donald Trump announced in April but neither ⁠company has confirmed.

content.title: Intel results to test if AI-fueled rally has room to run
content.summary: Intel's results on Thursday will show whether the American chip icon has the numbers to back a Wall Street rally that has ‌sent its shares nearly three times higher this year, as its turnaround ‌push wins over investors and the AI buildout powers demand.  Investors are also eager for details on potential new customers for its contract manufacturing business, which has shown growing traction with deals including a ‌Tesla tie-up.  • Among the biggest ⁠questions for Intel is a potential deal to manufacture processors for Apple that U.S. President Donald Trump announced in April but neither ⁠company has confirmed.
